File:Musubiishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2013.4.05 - panoramio.jpg|Musubiishi File:Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg === Setsusha === * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}) * Oyamazumi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamazumi Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}) * Inari Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Inari Okami|1=Ukanomitama}}) * Soreisha (ancestral spirits of the six families of Kawakami) === Main festivals === * Annual festival ({{ill|lt=June 1st|Q2625|WD=Q2625|1=June 1}}) - On that day, there is a custom in which each parishioner's household offers a {{ill|lt=mackerel|Q8064278|WD=Q8064278|lt=mackerel|1=Mackerel}} shaped sushi wrapped in {{ill|lt=Magnificent|Magnificent|WD=Magnificent|ltq=Q2386378}} leaves as a {{ill|lt=divine meal|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}}, which is called a ``human offering.'' * Taiko (Old) Dance - There is a ritual performing art that is said to have its origins in people who joyfully gathered in front of the shrine and danced after their prayers for rain came true, and it was designated as an intangible folk cultural property by the prefecture in 2001. === Cultural assets === === Nara Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Taiko (old) dance === The company name is a reference to {{ill|lt=Nyu Kawakami Shrine Kamisha|Q10877366|WD=Q10877366|1=Niukawakami Upper Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Kawakami Village (Nara Prefecture)|Q1204424|WD=Q1204424|lt=Kawakami Village|1=Kawakami, Nara}}. === Enshrined God === * '''{{ill|lt=Kuraokamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Yamikami|1=Kuraokami}}''' (Kuraokami no Kami) Previously, it was {{ill|lt=Nakamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takanijin|1=Kuraokami}}, but in the Taisho era, the enshrined deity was changed to {{ill|lt=Enshrined deity|Q11591102|WD=Q11591102|1=Changes to deities in the Meiji period}}, and it became the current enshrined deity. The enshrined deity is "Nyu Daimyojin", {{ill|lt=Nyutsuhime Shrine|Q705154|WD=Q705154|1=Niutsuhime Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{jal There is also a theory that it refers to Nyutsuhime, a deity enshrined in ink|Ito County|Q1131309}}{{ill|lt=Katsuragi Town|Q1345594|WD=Q1345594|1=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}). === History === The origins of Joko are unknown, but according to an old local legend, the torii gate of Nyu-sha Shrine was washed away by a flood, so Soshu was the one who picked it up and enshrined it as a divine object, according to the ``{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (1925) compiled by the Ministry of Education. However, it is unclear which shrine "Nyusha" refers to. is {{jalink|Nyuyama behind this shrine. (Nara Prefecture)||At the summit of Mt. Niu, there is a group of rectangular stones that appear to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ritual ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the {{ill|lt=Nyu River|Q11368596|WD=Q11368596|1=Nyu River}} basin that flows in front of the shrine is dotted with Niu Shrines. In addition, there were many wells such as Misakai, Goshikii, Fukibubu, Mishokui, Kigani, Mitarai, Hozojo, Kourin, and Kajini, which suggests that there may have been a {{ill|lt=Water God|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}} belief from ancient times.{{ill|lt=Edo Period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} From the early period onward, as historical research on the location of Shikinai Shrine became more active, the theory that "Shikiuchi Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine" was compared to this shrine, which was called "Nyu Daimyojin" at the time, became popular.This shrine was first identified in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Enlightenment|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Handbook|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Soin Shirai|1=UNKNOWN}}. . After that, {{ill|lt=Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}} and {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}} began to recognize this, and in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}) {{ill|lt=Emperor Nakamikado|Q314817|WD=Q314817|1=Emperor Nakamikado}}'s {{ill|lt=imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} was sent, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prayers for rain|1=UNKNOWN}} were sometimes {{ill|lt=hohei|Q11445508|WD=Q11445508|1=Hōhei}}, and {{jali nk|Kaei|Q1146125}} When the {{ill|lt=black ships|Q1136943|WD=Q1136943|1=Black Ships}} arrived in the year 6 ({{ill|lt=1853|Q7666|WD=Q7666|1=1853}}), the {{ill|lt=Emperor Komei|Q299179|WD=Q299179|1=Emperor Kōmei}} visited our shrine in the following year. |Proclamation|Q11452942}} and prayed for national security, and in {{ill|lt=Bunkyu|Q730266|WD=Q730266|1=Bunkyū}}2 ({{ill|lt=1862|Q7698|WD=Q7698|1=1862}}), he prayed for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Joi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and was treated as one of the 22 companies. In the 3rd year of Bunkyu ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701|1=1863}}), when the {{ill|lt=Tenchu-gumi|Q11443583|WD=Q11443583|1=Tenchūgumi}} uprising broke out, the rebels named Wakasa Hashimoto and Echizen Nakai As members of this shrine {{ill|lt=shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}} participated in this, the main shrine was damaged by the fire of the subjugation army, and the hall of worship and the shrine office were burned down.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}}), he was enshrined in Kanpei Taisha Shrine, but in 1873 (Meiji 6), the then junior chief priest {{jali nk|Masumi Eto}}, the enshrined place of this shrine was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dajokanfu|1=UNKNOWN}} (cited in ``{{ill|lt=Ruiju Sandaikaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'') Pointing out that it does not conform to the four solitude boundaries of Nyukawakami Shrine described in ``Okinki Yamato Province Nyukawakami Amashi Shrine Kaiji Jiji.'' At that time, Takaran Shrine (currently Kami-sha) was compared to Shikinai Nyukawakami Shrine, and in the following year, 1874, the shrine was called ``Kuchinomiya,'' and Takaran Shrine was called ``Oku no Miya,'' and it came to have jurisdiction over it. Later, the Eto theory was accepted, and in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29), this shrine was renamed ``Nyukawakami-jinja Shimosha,'' the inner shrine was renamed ``Dojosha,'' and the two shrines were combined to become ``Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine.''However, in 1915 ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) , Narakichi Moriguchi, a native of Higashiyoshino Village, where the current Chusha is located, wrote ``Nyukawakami Shrine Thoughts.'' Ikukawakami Shrine (Chusha)}}) = Nyukawakami editorial, which was accepted, {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taisho 11) 1 On October 12th, a notification from the Ministry of Home Affairs stated, ``Gosha Nyukawakami Shrine, enshrined in Ogawa Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, enshrined deity of the elephant goddess. Ukanpei Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine Chusha Toden Meraruru purport is enshrined.'' As a result, Kami-sha and Shimo-sha were incorporated into Chu-sha, and the three shrines were renamed Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine, and the deity enshrined at this shrine was also changed from Takayoku-jin to Yami-kami-jin. After World War II, it became independent in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}27), and currently belongs to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, and is considered its {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}. === Shinto priest === Until the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), there were hereditary Shinto priests (Shake) called ``Kubunsho'' or ``Shinin.'' According to the company journal, its origins date back to the {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} years (1469 - 1487), but in the early modern period, six families (known as the ``Six Kawakami'' because their real surname was Kawakami) served the service: Hashimoto, Inui, Kawago, Nakai, Mukai, and Imanishi. Although he left his job at the shrine during the government system era, his descendants have continued to be involved in the shrine as ``ceremonial personnel.'' === Precincts === * '''Shrine building''' :: The main shrine is {{ill|lt=Nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Sangensha Nagaru-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, and {{ill|lt=Chigi/Katsuoki|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234|1=chigi and katsuogi}} is placed on the ridge. It was rebuilt in 1885 ({{ill|lt=1885|Q7821|WD=Q7821|1=1885}}) after it was damaged by the war during the subjugation of the Tenchu-gumi in 1885. The worship hall, which is located about 70 wooden steps down from the main hall with a roof, is a {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} flat building with 5 rooms in the front and 2 rooms on the sides.It was also destroyed in the Tenchu-gumi Incident and was rebuilt in 1902. Furthermore, all of the shrine buildings have been reroofed in recent years with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate|1=UNKNOWN}}. * '''Sacred Horse'''' - According to the shrine's legend, the imperial court once dedicated a ``black horse'' when praying for rain, and a ``white horse'' when praying for sunshine. This water god belief is said to be the origin of ema, and was passed down to {{ill|lt=Kifune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|1=Kifune Shrine}} in Kyoto, according to the locally installed information board ``The Shrine of the Origin of Ema.'' . During the Muromachi period, the sacred horse presentation festival was discontinued, but due to frequent floods in recent years, the sacred horse presentation festival was held in 2012 for the first time in about 600 years in hopes of recovery.* '''Gyu-ishi/Frog-ishi''' - Gyu-ishi began to be called ox-ishi at some point because its shape resembles that of a cow. It was brought up from the Nyu River using only human power by young people from Nyu Ward and dedicated to commemorate the dedication of Emperor Taisho's grand ceremony.According to the locally installed information board ``Thoughts on Ushiki.'' . Frog Stone was named Frog Stone because it is shaped like an upright frog. . Cows walk slowly and tenaciously, meaning that their life and business should be like that of a cow, while frogs have the explosive power to grasp things in an instant, so stones with the contrasting characteristics of the stillness of a cow and the movement of a frog are placed side by side. * ``Musubiishi'' (Musubiishi) - The phallic and female genital objects overlap, and there is a hole about 10 centimeters deep in the stone. A believer who visited the shrine and was blessed with a child dedicated a stone that was found at the bottom of the Nyu River flowing in front of the shrine when he performed purification. According to the information board ``Musubiishi'' installed at the site. . * '''Mikenoi'' (Mikenoi) - sacred water "water of life" File:Torii of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Otorii at the entrance to the shrine grounds File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha Haiden.jpg|Shrine of worship File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha stairs2.jpg|75 covered floors (kizahashi) connecting the worship hall and File:Musubiishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2013.4.05 - panoramio.jpg|Musubiishi File:Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg === Setsusha === * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}) * Oyamazumi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamazumi Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}) * Inari Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Inari Okami|1=Ukanomitama}}) * Soreisha (ancestral spirits of the six families of Kawakami) === Main festivals === * Annual festival ({{ill|lt=June 1st|Q2625|WD=Q2625|1=June 1}}) - On that day, there is a custom in which each parishioner's household offers a {{ill|lt=mackerel|Q8064278|WD=Q8064278|lt=mackerel|1=Mackerel}} shaped sushi wrapped in {{ill|lt=Magnificent|Magnificent|WD=Magnificent|ltq=Q2386378}} leaves as a {{ill|lt=divine meal|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}}, which is called a ``human offering.'' * Taiko (Old) Dance - There is a ritual performing art that is said to have its origins in people who joyfully gathered in front of the shrine and danced after their prayers for rain came true, and it was designated as an intangible folk cultural property by the prefecture in 2001. === Cultural assets === === Nara Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Taiko (old) dance === {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Sangensha Ruzo|1=Nagare-zukuri}}Copper plate roof |Temples, etc. = {{ill|lt=Road of pilgrimage to sacred sites of Buddhism and Shintoism|Q10444949|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}Nara No. 29 (from summer 2018) |Alias = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nyu Daimyojin|1=UNKNOWN}} |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=June 1st|Q2625|WD=Q2625|1=June 1}} |Shinto ritual = Taiko (old) dance |Map = Japan Nara }}'''Niu Kawakami Shrine Shimosha'' (Niu Kawakami Shrine Shimosha) is {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Shimoichi Town|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}{{jalink|Hase {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in (Shimoichi Town)||Nagatani. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and {{ill|lt=Nijunisha|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (Shimohachisha) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}. {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system#government shrine|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}However, from 1921, it was included in the current Nyukawakami Shrine (Chusha). , currently {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shrine Headquarters|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. The company name is a reference to {{ill|lt=Nyu Kawakami Shrine Kamisha|Q10877366|WD=Q10877366|1=Niukawakami Upper Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Kawakami Village (Nara Prefecture)|Q1204424|WD=Q1204424|lt=Kawakami Village|1=Kawakami, Nara}}. === Enshrined God === * '''{{ill|lt=Kuraokamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Yamikami|1=Kuraokami}}''' (Kuraokami no Kami) Previously, it was {{ill|lt=Nakamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takanijin|1=Kuraokami}}, but in the Taisho era, the enshrined deity was changed to {{ill|lt=Enshrined deity|Q11591102|WD=Q11591102|1=Changes to deities in the Meiji period}}, and it became the current enshrined deity. The enshrined deity is "Nyu Daimyojin", {{ill|lt=Nyutsuhime Shrine|Q705154|WD=Q705154|1=Niutsuhime Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{jal There is also a theory that it refers to Nyutsuhime, a deity enshrined in ink|Ito County|Q1131309}}{{ill|lt=Katsuragi Town|Q1345594|WD=Q1345594|1=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}). === History === The origins of Joko are unknown, but according to an old local legend, the torii gate of Nyu-sha Shrine was washed away by a flood, so Soshu was the one who picked it up and enshrined it as a divine object, according to the ``{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (1925) compiled by the Ministry of Education. However, it is unclear which shrine "Nyusha" refers to. is {{jalink|Nyuyama behind this shrine. (Nara Prefecture)||At the summit of Mt. Niu, there is a group of rectangular stones that appear to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ritual ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the {{ill|lt=Nyu River|Q11368596|WD=Q11368596|1=Nyu River}} basin that flows in front of the shrine is dotted with Niu Shrines. In addition, there were many wells such as Misakai, Goshikii, Fukibubu, Mishokui, Kigani, Mitarai, Hozojo, Kourin, and Kajini, which suggests that there may have been a {{ill|lt=Water God|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}} belief from ancient times.{{ill|lt=Edo Period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} From the early period onward, as historical research on the location of Shikinai Shrine became more active, the theory that "Shikiuchi Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine" was compared to this shrine, which was called "Nyu Daimyojin" at the time, became popular.This shrine was first identified in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Enlightenment|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Handbook|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Soin Shirai|1=UNKNOWN}}. . After that, {{ill|lt=Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}} and {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}} began to recognize this, and in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}) {{ill|lt=Emperor Nakamikado|Q314817|WD=Q314817|1=Emperor Nakamikado}}'s {{ill|lt=imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} was sent, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prayers for rain|1=UNKNOWN}} were sometimes {{ill|lt=hohei|Q11445508|WD=Q11445508|1=Hōhei}}, and {{jali nk|Kaei|Q1146125}} When the {{ill|lt=black ships|Q1136943|WD=Q1136943|1=Black Ships}} arrived in the year 6 ({{ill|lt=1853|Q7666|WD=Q7666|1=1853}}), the {{ill|lt=Emperor Komei|Q299179|WD=Q299179|1=Emperor Kōmei}} visited our shrine in the following year. |Proclamation|Q11452942}} and prayed for national security, and in {{ill|lt=Bunkyu|Q730266|WD=Q730266|1=Bunkyū}}2 ({{ill|lt=1862|Q7698|WD=Q7698|1=1862}}), he prayed for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Joi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and was treated as one of the 22 companies. In the 3rd year of Bunkyu ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701|1=1863}}), when the {{ill|lt=Tenchu-gumi|Q11443583|WD=Q11443583|1=Tenchūgumi}} uprising broke out, the rebels named Wakasa Hashimoto and Echizen Nakai As members of this shrine {{ill|lt=shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}} participated in this, the main shrine was damaged by the fire of the subjugation army, and the hall of worship and the shrine office were burned down.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}}), he was enshrined in Kanpei Taisha Shrine, but in 1873 (Meiji 6), the then junior chief priest {{jali nk|Masumi Eto}}, the enshrined place of this shrine was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dajokanfu|1=UNKNOWN}} (cited in ``{{ill|lt=Ruiju Sandaikaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'') Pointing out that it does not conform to the four solitude boundaries of Nyukawakami Shrine described in ``Okinki Yamato Province Nyukawakami Amashi Shrine Kaiji Jiji.'' At that time, Takaran Shrine (currently Kami-sha) was compared to Shikinai Nyukawakami Shrine, and in the following year, 1874, the shrine was called ``Kuchinomiya,'' and Takaran Shrine was called ``Oku no Miya,'' and it came to have jurisdiction over it. Later, the Eto theory was accepted, and in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29), this shrine was renamed ``Nyukawakami-jinja Shimosha,'' the inner shrine was renamed ``Dojosha,'' and the two shrines were combined to become ``Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine.''However, in 1915 ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) , Narakichi Moriguchi, a native of Higashiyoshino Village, where the current Chusha is located, wrote ``Nyukawakami Shrine Thoughts.'' Ikukawakami Shrine (Chusha)}}) = Nyukawakami editorial, which was accepted, {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taisho 11) 1 On October 12th, a notification from the Ministry of Home Affairs stated, ``Gosha Nyukawakami Shrine, enshrined in Ogawa Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, enshrined deity of the elephant goddess. Ukanpei Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine Chusha Toden Meraruru purport is enshrined.'' As a result, Kami-sha and Shimo-sha were incorporated into Chu-sha, and the three shrines were renamed Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine, and the deity enshrined at this shrine was also changed from Takayoku-jin to Yami-kami-jin. After World War II, it became independent in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}27), and currently belongs to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, and is considered its {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}. === Shinto priest === Until the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), there were hereditary Shinto priests (Shake) called ``Kubunsho'' or ``Shinin.'' According to the company journal, its origins date back to the {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} years (1469 - 1487), but in the early modern period, six families (known as the ``Six Kawakami'' because their real surname was Kawakami) served the service: Hashimoto, Inui, Kawago, Nakai, Mukai, and Imanishi. Although he left his job at the shrine during the government system era, his descendants have continued to be involved in the shrine as ``ceremonial personnel.'' === Precincts === * '''Shrine building''' :: The main shrine is {{ill|lt=Nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Sangensha Nagaru-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, and {{ill|lt=Chigi/Katsuoki|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234|1=chigi and katsuogi}} is placed on the ridge. It was rebuilt in 1885 ({{ill|lt=1885|Q7821|WD=Q7821|1=1885}}) after it was damaged by the war during the subjugation of the Tenchu-gumi in 1885. The worship hall, which is located about 70 wooden steps down from the main hall with a roof, is a {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} flat building with 5 rooms in the front and 2 rooms on the sides.It was also destroyed in the Tenchu-gumi Incident and was rebuilt in 1902. Furthermore, all of the shrine buildings have been reroofed in recent years with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate|1=UNKNOWN}}. * '''Sacred Horse'''' - According to the shrine's legend, the imperial court once dedicated a ``black horse'' when praying for rain, and a ``white horse'' when praying for sunshine. This water god belief is said to be the origin of ema, and was passed down to {{ill|lt=Kifune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|1=Kifune Shrine}} in Kyoto, according to the locally installed information board ``The Shrine of the Origin of Ema.'' . During the Muromachi period, the sacred horse presentation festival was discontinued, but due to frequent floods in recent years, the sacred horse presentation festival was held in 2012 for the first time in about 600 years in hopes of recovery.* '''Gyu-ishi/Frog-ishi''' - Gyu-ishi began to be called ox-ishi at some point because its shape resembles that of a cow. It was brought up from the Nyu River using only human power by young people from Nyu Ward and dedicated to commemorate the dedication of Emperor Taisho's grand ceremony.According to the locally installed information board ``Thoughts on Ushiki.'' . Frog Stone was named Frog Stone because it is shaped like an upright frog. . Cows walk slowly and tenaciously, meaning that their life and business should be like that of a cow, while frogs have the explosive power to grasp things in an instant, so stones with the contrasting characteristics of the stillness of a cow and the movement of a frog are placed side by side. * ``Musubiishi'' (Musubiishi) - The phallic and female genital objects overlap, and there is a hole about 10 centimeters deep in the stone. A believer who visited the shrine and was blessed with a child dedicated a stone that was found at the bottom of the Nyu River flowing in front of the shrine when he performed purification. According to the information board ``Musubiishi'' installed at the site. . * '''Mikenoi'' (Mikenoi) - sacred water "water of life" File:Torii of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Otorii at the entrance to the shrine grounds File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha Haiden.jpg|Shrine of worship File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha stairs2.jpg|75 covered floors (kizahashi) connecting the worship hall and main shrineFile:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Shinba File: Ushi-ishi and Frog-ishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2010.2.27 - panoramio.jpg|Ushi-ishi (left) and Frog-ishi (right) File:Musubiishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2013.4.05 - panoramio.jpg|Musubiishi File:Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg === Setsusha === * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}) * Oyamazumi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamazumi Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}) * Inari Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Inari Okami|1=Ukanomitama}}) * Soreisha (ancestral spirits of the six families of Kawakami) === Main festivals === * Annual festival ({{ill|lt=June 1st|Q2625|WD=Q2625|1=June 1}}) - On that day, there is a custom in which each parishioner's household offers a {{ill|lt=mackerel|Q8064278|WD=Q8064278|lt=mackerel|1=Mackerel}} shaped sushi wrapped in {{ill|lt=Magnificent|Magnificent|WD=Magnificent|ltq=Q2386378}} leaves as a {{ill|lt=divine meal|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}}, which is called a ``human offering.'' * Taiko (Old) Dance - There is a ritual performing art that is said to have its origins in people who joyfully gathered in front of the shrine and danced after their prayers for rain came true, and it was designated as an intangible folk cultural property by the prefecture in 2001. === Cultural assets === === Nara Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Taiko (old) dance === The company name is a reference to {{ill|lt=Nyu Kawakami Shrine Kamisha|Q10877366|WD=Q10877366|1=Niukawakami Upper Shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Kawakami Village (Nara Prefecture)|Q1204424|WD=Q1204424|lt=Kawakami Village|1=Kawakami, Nara}}. === Enshrined God === * '''{{ill|lt=Kuraokamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Yamikami|1=Kuraokami}}''' (Kuraokami no Kami) Previously, it was {{ill|lt=Nakamikami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takanijin|1=Kuraokami}}, but in the Taisho era, the enshrined deity was changed to {{ill|lt=Enshrined deity|Q11591102|WD=Q11591102|1=Changes to deities in the Meiji period}}, and it became the current enshrined deity. The enshrined deity is "Nyu Daimyojin", {{ill|lt=Nyutsuhime Shrine|Q705154|WD=Q705154|1=Niutsuhime Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{jal There is also a theory that it refers to Nyutsuhime, a deity enshrined in ink|Ito County|Q1131309}}{{ill|lt=Katsuragi Town|Q1345594|WD=Q1345594|1=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}). === History === The origins of Joko are unknown, but according to an old local legend, the torii gate of Nyu-sha Shrine was washed away by a flood, so Soshu was the one who picked it up and enshrined it as a divine object, according to the ``{{ill|lt=Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (1925) compiled by the Ministry of Education. However, it is unclear which shrine "Nyusha" refers to. is {{jalink|Nyuyama behind this shrine. (Nara Prefecture)||At the summit of Mt. Niu, there is a group of rectangular stones that appear to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ritual ruins|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the {{ill|lt=Nyu River|Q11368596|WD=Q11368596|1=Nyu River}} basin that flows in front of the shrine is dotted with Niu Shrines. In addition, there were many wells such as Misakai, Goshikii, Fukibubu, Mishokui, Kigani, Mitarai, Hozojo, Kourin, and Kajini, which suggests that there may have been a {{ill|lt=Water God|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}} belief from ancient times.{{ill|lt=Edo Period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} From the early period onward, as historical research on the location of Shikinai Shrine became more active, the theory that "Shikiuchi Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine" was compared to this shrine, which was called "Nyu Daimyojin" at the time, became popular.This shrine was first identified in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Enlightenment|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shrine Handbook|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Soin Shirai|1=UNKNOWN}}. . After that, {{ill|lt=Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}} and {{ill|lt=Edo Shogunate|Q205662|WD=Q205662|lt=Bakufu|1=Tokugawa shogunate}} began to recognize this, and in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1710|Q6861|WD=Q6861|1=1710}}) {{ill|lt=Emperor Nakamikado|Q314817|WD=Q314817|1=Emperor Nakamikado}}'s {{ill|lt=imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920|1=Chokushi}} was sent, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prayers for rain|1=UNKNOWN}} were sometimes {{ill|lt=hohei|Q11445508|WD=Q11445508|1=Hōhei}}, and {{jali nk|Kaei|Q1146125}} When the {{ill|lt=black ships|Q1136943|WD=Q1136943|1=Black Ships}} arrived in the year 6 ({{ill|lt=1853|Q7666|WD=Q7666|1=1853}}), the {{ill|lt=Emperor Komei|Q299179|WD=Q299179|1=Emperor Kōmei}} visited our shrine in the following year. |Proclamation|Q11452942}} and prayed for national security, and in {{ill|lt=Bunkyu|Q730266|WD=Q730266|1=Bunkyū}}2 ({{ill|lt=1862|Q7698|WD=Q7698|1=1862}}), he prayed for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Joi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and was treated as one of the 22 companies. In the 3rd year of Bunkyu ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701|1=1863}}), when the {{ill|lt=Tenchu-gumi|Q11443583|WD=Q11443583|1=Tenchūgumi}} uprising broke out, the rebels named Wakasa Hashimoto and Echizen Nakai As members of this shrine {{ill|lt=shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}} participated in this, the main shrine was damaged by the fire of the subjugation army, and the hall of worship and the shrine office were burned down.In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}}), he was enshrined in Kanpei Taisha Shrine, but in 1873 (Meiji 6), the then junior chief priest {{jali nk|Masumi Eto}}, the enshrined place of this shrine was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dajokanfu|1=UNKNOWN}} (cited in ``{{ill|lt=Ruiju Sandaikaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'') Pointing out that it does not conform to the four solitude boundaries of Nyukawakami Shrine described in ``Okinki Yamato Province Nyukawakami Amashi Shrine Kaiji Jiji.'' At that time, Takaran Shrine (currently Kami-sha) was compared to Shikinai Nyukawakami Shrine, and in the following year, 1874, the shrine was called ``Kuchinomiya,'' and Takaran Shrine was called ``Oku no Miya,'' and it came to have jurisdiction over it. Later, the Eto theory was accepted, and in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29), this shrine was renamed ``Nyukawakami-jinja Shimosha,'' the inner shrine was renamed ``Dojosha,'' and the two shrines were combined to become ``Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine.''However, in 1915 ({{ill|lt=Taisho|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}4) , Narakichi Moriguchi, a native of Higashiyoshino Village, where the current Chusha is located, wrote ``Nyukawakami Shrine Thoughts.'' Ikukawakami Shrine (Chusha)}}) = Nyukawakami editorial, which was accepted, {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taisho 11) 1 On October 12th, a notification from the Ministry of Home Affairs stated, ``Gosha Nyukawakami Shrine, enshrined in Ogawa Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, enshrined deity of the elephant goddess. Ukanpei Taisha Nyukawakami Shrine Chusha Toden Meraruru purport is enshrined.'' As a result, Kami-sha and Shimo-sha were incorporated into Chu-sha, and the three shrines were renamed Kanpei Taisha Nyukawakami-jinja Shrine, and the deity enshrined at this shrine was also changed from Takayoku-jin to Yami-kami-jin. After World War II, it became independent in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}27), and currently belongs to {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}, and is considered its {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}. === Shinto priest === Until the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), there were hereditary Shinto priests (Shake) called ``Kubunsho'' or ``Shinin.'' According to the company journal, its origins date back to the {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} years (1469 - 1487), but in the early modern period, six families (known as the ``Six Kawakami'' because their real surname was Kawakami) served the service: Hashimoto, Inui, Kawago, Nakai, Mukai, and Imanishi. Although he left his job at the shrine during the government system era, his descendants have continued to be involved in the shrine as ``ceremonial personnel.'' === Precincts === * '''Shrine building''' :: The main shrine is {{ill|lt=Nagaru-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Sangensha Nagaru-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, and {{ill|lt=Chigi/Katsuoki|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234|1=chigi and katsuogi}} is placed on the ridge. It was rebuilt in 1885 ({{ill|lt=1885|Q7821|WD=Q7821|1=1885}}) after it was damaged by the war during the subjugation of the Tenchu-gumi in 1885. The worship hall, which is located about 70 wooden steps down from the main hall with a roof, is a {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} flat building with 5 rooms in the front and 2 rooms on the sides.It was also destroyed in the Tenchu-gumi Incident and was rebuilt in 1902. Furthermore, all of the shrine buildings have been reroofed in recent years with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper plate|1=UNKNOWN}}. * '''Sacred Horse'''' - According to the shrine's legend, the imperial court once dedicated a ``black horse'' when praying for rain, and a ``white horse'' when praying for sunshine. This water god belief is said to be the origin of ema, and was passed down to {{ill|lt=Kifune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|1=Kifune Shrine}} in Kyoto, according to the locally installed information board ``The Shrine of the Origin of Ema.'' . During the Muromachi period, the sacred horse presentation festival was discontinued, but due to frequent floods in recent years, the sacred horse presentation festival was held in 2012 for the first time in about 600 years in hopes of recovery.* '''Gyu-ishi/Frog-ishi''' - Gyu-ishi began to be called ox-ishi at some point because its shape resembles that of a cow. It was brought up from the Nyu River using only human power by young people from Nyu Ward and dedicated to commemorate the dedication of Emperor Taisho's grand ceremony.According to the locally installed information board ``Thoughts on Ushiki.'' . Frog Stone was named Frog Stone because it is shaped like an upright frog. . Cows walk slowly and tenaciously, meaning that their life and business should be like that of a cow, while frogs have the explosive power to grasp things in an instant, so stones with the contrasting characteristics of the stillness of a cow and the movement of a frog are placed side by side. * ``Musubiishi'' (Musubiishi) - The phallic and female genital objects overlap, and there is a hole about 10 centimeters deep in the stone. A believer who visited the shrine and was blessed with a child dedicated a stone that was found at the bottom of the Nyu River flowing in front of the shrine when he performed purification. According to the information board ``Musubiishi'' installed at the site. . * '''Mikenoi'' (Mikenoi) - sacred water "water of life" File:Torii of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Otorii at the entrance to the shrine grounds File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha Haiden.jpg|Shrine of worship File:Niukawakami-jinja shimosha stairs2.jpg|75 covered floors (kizahashi) connecting the worship hall and main shrineFile:Niukawakami-shimosha 201905 Shinme.jpg|Shinba File: Ushi-ishi and Frog-ishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2010.2.27 - panoramio.jpg|Ushi-ishi (left) and Frog-ishi (right) File:Musubiishi at Nyukawakami Shrine Shimosha 2013.4.05 - panoramio.jpg|Musubiishi File:Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg|Well (Sacred water) of Niukawakami-jinja Shimosha 001.jpg === Setsusha === * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}) * Oyamazumi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Oyamazumi Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}) * Inari Shrine ({{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Inari Okami|1=Ukanomitama}}) * Soreisha (ancestral spirits of the six families of Kawakami) === Main festivals === * Annual festival ({{ill|lt=June 1st|Q2625|WD=Q2625|1=June 1}}) - On that day, there is a custom in which each parishioner's household offers a {{ill|lt=mackerel|Q8064278|WD=Q8064278|lt=mackerel|1=Mackerel}} shaped sushi wrapped in {{ill|lt=Magnificent|Magnificent|WD=Magnificent|ltq=Q2386378}} leaves as a {{ill|lt=divine meal|Q11591045|WD=Q11591045|1=Shinsen}}, which is called a ``human offering.'' * Taiko (Old) Dance - There is a ritual performing art that is said to have its origins in people who joyfully gathered in front of the shrine and danced after their prayers for rain came true, and it was designated as an intangible folk cultural property by the prefecture in 2001. === Cultural assets === === Nara Prefecture designated intangible folk cultural property === * Taiko (old) dance ===
